document.title: Software Validation Report
standards: FDA Quality System Regulation 21 CFR, Part 820
FDA Quality System Regulation 21 CFR, Part 803
CDRH Guidance: General Principles of Software Validation
CDRH Guidance: Off-The-Shelf Software Use in Medical Devices
CDRH Guidance: Guidance for the Submission of Premarket Notifications for Medical Image Management Devices
IEEE 1012-1986, Standard for Software Verification and Validation Plans
IEEE 829-1983, Standard for Software Test Documentation

sourceSummary.oneLine: Software validation report A-TCF-5-010 Rev.2 for Digital Flat Panel X-ray Detector models HAD1717MC and HAD1717MG.
sourceSummary.officialUse: Supports software validation documentation with SRS, SDS, hazard analysis, test plan, traceability, V&V results, and software version history.
keyPoints: The software level of concern is defined as Moderate (Class B).
The software is described as middleware between the still image detector and the End User Application.
The report references FDA/CDRH guidance, quality system regulations, and medical device software standards including IEC 62304 and EN ISO 14971.
Static and dynamic tests are reported as approved or passed, with no unsolved safety or effectiveness bugs found.
Software version 1.0.0 and firmware version 1.0 are listed as initial releases in March 2019.

evidence.claim: The document is Software Validation Report A-TCF-5-010 Rev.2 dated March/31/2020.
evidence.evidenceText: Software Validation Report File No A-TCF-5-010 Rev. No 2 Date March/31/2020

evidence.claim: The report applies to Digital Flat Panel X-ray Detector models HAD1717MC and HAD1717MG.
evidence.evidenceText: For Digital Flat Panel X-ray Detector HAD1717MC, HAD1717MG

evidence.claim: Revision 2 was issued on March/31/2020 for correction of audit points.
evidence.evidenceText: 2 March/31/2020 Correction of audit Points
evidence.pageTarget: Software Validation Report A-TCF-5-010 Revision History

evidence.claim: The software level of concern is Moderate (Class B).
evidence.evidenceText: 1. Level of concern: Moderate (Class B)
evidence.pageTarget: Medical Device Software Level of Concern

evidence.claim: The level of concern rationale states that TFT-detectors use low X-ray irradiation and the level is moderate.
evidence.evidenceText: TFT-detectors use low X-ray irradiation that could not harm patient seriously in any way. Because of that the level of concern is defined as “moderate”.
evidence.pageTarget: Medical Device Software Level of Concern

evidence.claim: The term Software in the report refers to an acquisition library for detectors.
evidence.evidenceText: The term “Software” when used from uppercase “S” in respective context of this document assumes this “acquisition library for detectors”.

evidence.claim: The software is a middleware utility library between a still image detector and the End User Application.
evidence.evidenceText: This is “middle-ware” utility library between still image detector (and its low-level driver) from one side and EUA from the other.

evidence.claim: The software provides setup functions for configuration, gain/offset calibration, and bad pixel correction preparation.
evidence.evidenceText: It also contains SU to modify software and hardware configuration parameters and preparation of Gain/Offset-Calibration and Bad Pixels’ Correction.

evidence.claim: Referenced regulatory standards and guidances include FDA 21 CFR Part 820, FDA 21 CFR Part 803, and CDRH software guidance.
evidence.evidenceText: FDA Quality System Regulation 21 CFR, Part 820; FDA Quality System Regulation 21 CFR, Part 803; CDRH Guidance: General Principles of Software Validation

evidence.claim: Referenced industry standards include EN ISO 13485, IEC 60601-1, IEC 62304, and EN ISO14971.
The development environment includes Windows 10 Pro 64bit and MS Visual C++ 2019.
evidence.evidenceText: Development Environment (SRS-C-DevE): Windows 10 pro 64bit. MS Visual C++ 2019 (MS Visual Studio Professional 2019).

evidence.claim: The detector operational environment includes Ubuntu 14.0 Linux and a VMware virtual machine connection.
evidence.evidenceText: Detector Operational Environment Peripheral hardware : Window10 connected by VMware virtual machine OS : Ubuntu 14.0 Linux

evidence.claim: Hardware and interface requirements include a TFT sensor connected by Ethernet and at least Windows 7 32-bit/64-bit.
evidence.evidenceText: Hardware and interface requirement Peripheral hardware TFT sensor connected by Ethernet OS : At least Window 7 32-bit/64-bit.

evidence.claim: SRS 1 acquisition requires acquiring a raw frame from the detector and delivering it to the EUA as an image.
evidence.evidenceText: SRS 1 Acquisition Acquire raw frame from detector and deliver it to EUA as image.

evidence.claim: Offset/Gain Calibration equalizes transfer functions of each pixel and supports Flat-Field Correction.
evidence.evidenceText: Offset/Gain-Calibration This operation equalizes transfer functions of each pixel. It shall be at least Flat-Field Correction (FFC) with one flat bright calibration point and one dark frame.

evidence.claim: Trigger modes include Line mode and AED mode.
evidence.evidenceText: SRS 1.8 Trigger mode Line mode, AED mode

evidence.claim: Line mode uses a trigger cable and AED mode does not.
evidence.evidenceText: The difference between the line mode and the AED mode is that the line mode uses the trigger cable, and the AED mode does not.

evidence.claim: The hazard analysis uses Failure Mode and Effects Analysis.
evidence.evidenceText: The model used for the Hazard Evaluation was Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA).

evidence.claim: The hazard summary includes mismatched or absent calibration file as a low-probability moderate concern hazard.
evidence.evidenceText: HZ 0001 Mismatched calibration file (No calibration file) L Moderate

evidence.claim: The hazard summary includes stopped data transmission after X-ray exposure due to LAN cable disconnection.
evidence.evidenceText: HZ 1001 Stop data-transmission after X-ray exposure because of disconnection of LAN-cable. L Moderate

evidence.claim: Static tests and dynamic tests are defined for software validation.
evidence.evidenceText: We classify the test by two groups as the next: Static– expert reviews and analysis of source code and DEBUG release of Software. Dynamic –run Software for test data acquisition and processing

evidence.claim: Static tests and analysis were approved.
evidence.evidenceText: Static Tests and Analysis Topic (subject) of test Result is Ok (approved)

evidence.claim: Dynamic tests TS 0002B, TS 0003B, TS 0004A, TS 0006A, TS 1001A, TS 1002A, and TS 1003A passed.
evidence.evidenceText: TS 0002B Passed; TS 0003B Passed; TS 0004A Passed; TS 0006A Passed; TS 1001A Passed; TS 1002A Passed; TS 1003A Passed

evidence.claim: The report states that no unsolved bugs subject to safety and effectiveness were found.
evidence.evidenceText: There were no found unsolved bugs that are subjected to safety and effectiveness in this software.

evidence.claim: Software version 1.0.0 was initially released on March/15/2019 for HAD-MD-SW.
evidence.evidenceText: 1.0.0 March/15/2019 HAD-MD-SW Initial release, Dual polynomial fitting calibration Only for proprietary applications.

evidence.claim: Firmware version 1.0 was initially released on March/12/2019 for HAD-MD-FW.
evidence.evidenceText: 1.0 March/12/2019 HAD-MD-FW Initial release

reviewFlags.reason: Authority context is ambiguous: the source path is under CE MDD materials, while the source metadata authoritySignal is FDA and the document references FDA/CDRH guidance.
Manufacturer or legal entity name is not clearly stated in the extracted source text.
OCR/text quality issues are present, including misspellings such as “Aril,” “Traceabilty,” and garbled diagram text.
